food_subgroup: Dry-braised spiced coconut beef with steamed rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Beef Rendang with Steamed Rice. Nearest parent is a braised meat dish with spiced coconut served with rice. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this canonical Malay dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Braised meat dishes — dry-braised beef in coconut milk and spices with steamed rice (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Beef Rendang. A03XB (braised meat dishes) is the closest defensible parent. A dedicated code for rendang-type dishes is recommended.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Beef Rendang. Not applicable for this traditional composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Beef Rendang with Steamed Rice weighs approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150–180 g of beef rendang and 220–250 g of steamed white rice, as commonly served at Malay hawker stalls and restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Rendang — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, Food Code F06040005
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Food Code F06040005 (Beef Rendang with steamed rice), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are from enriched_json derivation with confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central component data for beef and coconut milk.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white jasmine rice as primary GI contributor in the combined dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this combined dish. The high protein (11.5 g/100 g) and fat (10.5 g/100 g) content from beef and coconut milk, along with dietary fibre from spices and kerisik, attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load of 35.6 is calculated per 400 g standard serving: (55 × 16.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 400 g / 100) / 100 = 35.6. This is classified as a high GL per serving, reflecting the large rice portion.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 382 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1,528 mg sodium per standard 400 g serving (approximately 66% of the 2,300 mg daily recommended limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal and request reduced-salt preparation where possible, as a single standard serving provides approximately two-thirds of the recommended daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (~35.6 per standard 400 g serving) driven primarily by the steamed white rice component; the rendang itself is low in carbohydrates but high in saturated fat from coconut milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g and increase the rendang-to-rice ratio to lower the glycaemic load per meal, while monitoring total saturated fat intake from coconut milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content — 6.8 g per 100 g (approximately 27.2 g per 400 g serving), primarily from coconut milk and kerisik (toasted grated coconut), exceeding the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g for adults
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Beef Rendang to small portions (≤150 g) and infrequent occasions, as a standard serving provides saturated fat well above the daily recommended limit, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk may promote inflammation; however, turmeric and galangal in the spice paste provide curcumin and bioactive phytocompounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Beef Rendang in moderate portions, benefiting from the anti-inflammatory spices, but should be mindful of the high saturated fat content which may exacerbate systemic inflammation if consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Rendang with Steamed Rice is a canonical Malay and Indonesian composite dish consisting of slow-braised beef cooked in a rich, spiced coconut milk paste until nearly all liquid has evaporated, served over steamed white rice. The dish originates from the Minangkabau people of West Sumatra, Indonesia, and has been adopted as a signature festive and everyday dish across Malaysia and Singapore. The rendang component is prepared by simmering beef in coconut milk with a complex spice paste (rempah) comprising lemongrass, galangal, ginger, turmeric, garlic, shallots, and dried chilies, along with kaffir lime leaves and kerisik (toasted grated coconut). The prolonged slow-cooking process — often 3–4 hours — causes the coconut milk to reduce completely, allowing the beef to fry in the residual coconut oil, resulting in a dark, caramelised, intensely aromatic dry crust on the meat. The texture is tender and fibrous, with a deeply complex flavour profile combining richness from coconut fat, heat from chilies, earthiness from galangal and turmeric, and a distinctive nutty depth from kerisik. Nutritionally, the combined dish provides 192 kcal per 100 g, with 11.5 g protein, 10.5 g fat (6.8 g saturated), and 16.2 g carbohydrate. A standard 400 g plate serving delivers approximately 768 kcal, 46 g protein, 42 g fat, and 64.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~382 mg/100 g) and saturated fat, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease at full serving sizes. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Beef Rendang is a centrepiece of Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebrations and is widely available at Malay hawker centres and restaurants throughout Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Food Code F06040005 (Beef Rendang with steamed rice), an approved primary regional source, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated. Minor fields (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are derived from enriched_json with confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central component data. GI is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice as the primary carbohydrate contributor.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Beef Rendang with steamed rice is not separately reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the primary source. Copper (0.15 mg/100 g), manganese (0.25 mg/100 g), and vitamin_k (5.0 mcg/100 g) are derived from enriched_json with confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central component data for beef and coconut milk; these values are plausible but not directly verified from a primary tabulated source. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is a confirmed low value from the primary source. All core macros (protein_g 11.5, fat_g 10.5, carbohydrate_g 16.2) are non-zero and fully supported. Glycaemic load of 35.6 per 400 g serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 64.8) / 100 = 35.6, classified as high GL per serving. The GI of 55 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice, the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Portions: three portion sizes are documented — 400 g (1 plate, default, verified), 200 g (1 serving, enriched_json), and 180 g (1 side portion, grabfood). The 400 g plate is used as the standard serving for GL and health context calculations. This dish is a Singaporean and Malaysian Malay dish; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Indonesian per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules.
